summary: I was born in 1908 and have spent most of my life in the Reno area. From 1945 to 1964, I was deeply involved in the ski industry, notably building and operating the Sky Tavern Lodge at the Mt. Rose Ski Bowl, which was the first ski operation in that area. Besides running the ski lodge, I also had a successful contracting business during the summer months. I enjoyed skiing and organizing ski meets and races, and I took pride in developing ski facilities and courses around Mt. Rose and nearby locations.

Reno has been my home for most of my life. It’s where I ran my contracting business during the summers and where I lived while managing the Sky Tavern Lodge up at Mt. Rose. The Reno Army Air Base was nearby, and I even employed some of the GIs from there to help with construction projects at Sky Tavern. Reno has always been the hub for my work and life in the ski industry.

I built the Sky Tavern Lodge starting in September 1945 and opened it by December 15th that same year. It was the first ski operation in the Mt. Rose area, and I constructed it on land I purchased from Robison Neeman. The lodge started with minimal facilities but grew to include a large parking lot and sewage system, all built with the help of crews from Reno and GIs from the Reno Army Air Base. Later, in 1968, the lodge was remodeled for use by the junior ski program, which was a proud moment for me.

The Mount Rose Ski Bowl was the site where I operated the Sky Tavern Lodge and built ski lifts. It was an area with early ski activity, and I was involved in running ski meets and setting up race courses there. The land I owned included parts of what is now the Mount Rose Ski Area, and I witnessed its development from the 1940s through the 1960s. It was a challenging but rewarding place to work, especially with the weather and terrain conditions.

The University of Nevada, Reno, through its Oral History Program, interviewed me in 1983 to preserve my recollections about skiing and development in the Mt. Rose area. The program has been instrumental in documenting the history of Nevada and the West, and I was glad to contribute my experiences from 1945 to 1964. My oral history is now part of their archives, available for future researchers interested in the region's ski industry and development.

Edith S. Swift was the interviewer from the University of Nevada Oral History Program who conducted my oral history sessions in February and March of 1983. She asked detailed questions about the Sky Tavern Lodge, the ski industry, and my experiences building and running the ski operation. Her work helped me share my story clearly and preserve it for future generations.
cues: University of Nevada Oral History Program

summary: Robison Neeman was the person from whom I purchased the land for the Sky Tavern Lodge on May 25, 1945. At the time, the property had minimal ski facilities, including a T-bar installed in 1944. Neeman had previously operated a two-section rope tow for one season and had kicked off earlier rope tow operators. Buying this land was the first step in establishing the ski operation I developed.

During the construction of the Sky Tavern Lodge and its infrastructure, I employed GIs from the Reno Army Air Base to help with labor-intensive tasks like carrying water pipes and building the sewage system. They worked day shifts at the lodge and returned to the base for night shifts. Their assistance was crucial, especially given the labor shortages right after World War II.
cues: helped build water and sewage systems

summary: Part of the land I purchased for the Sky Tavern operation included acreage below Galena Creek at the edge of the timber. This was within the approximately 800 acres I owned in the Mt. Rose vicinity. The area was part of the broader landscape that supported the ski operations and development I was involved with in the mid-20th century.

The land I bought in 1945 included the parcel where Reindeer Lodge now stands. This was part of the larger acreage around Mt. Rose that I owned and managed. Though I did not build Reindeer Lodge myself, the property was connected to my ski area holdings and development efforts in the region.

Included in the land I purchased was a parcel known as the Christmas Tree area, which is now part of the Mount Rose Ski Area. This 280-acre section was above other parts of my property and contributed to the overall ski terrain available in the region. It was part of the land that supported the growth of skiing activities I was involved with from the mid-1940s onward.

Wayne Poulsen was one of the early pioneers in skiing in the Mt. Rose area. In 1939 and 1940, he and a partner named Ed Health installed a small rope tow on the hill, which was one of the first ski facilities before I developed Sky Tavern. Their efforts laid groundwork for skiing in the area, though their operation was eventually displaced by later owners.

I recall skiing at Sugar Bowl Ski Resort in the late 1930s, around 1938, when rope tows were just starting to appear. It was one of the places where skiing was developing in the region, and I had experience there before focusing on Mt. Rose. Sugar Bowl represented some of the earliest organized ski areas in the Sierra Nevada during that era.

Donner Summit was another early skiing location I was familiar with before developing Mt. Rose. In 1938, there was a rope tow installed there, and I skied in that area during the early days of skiing in the region. It was part of the broader Sierra Nevada ski scene that influenced my interest in the sport and ski business.

Lake Tahoe is near the areas where I developed ski operations, including Mt. Rose and Incline Village. The region around Lake Tahoe has long been a center for skiing and outdoor recreation, and my work contributed to the growth of skiing on the Nevada side of the lake. The natural beauty and snow conditions of the Tahoe area made it an ideal place for ski development.
cues: near Mt. Rose and Incline Village

summary: Incline Village is a community near the ski areas I was involved with, including Mt. Rose. While my main focus was on Sky Tavern and Mt. Rose Ski Bowl, Incline Village was part of the broader Lake Tahoe region where skiing was growing rapidly during the mid-20th century. It was an important neighboring area for winter sports enthusiasts.

Although not detailed extensively in my interview, the Reno Chamber of Commerce was part of the community context during my years operating the Sky Tavern Lodge. As a local businessman and contractor, I was connected to Reno’s business environment, which the Chamber supported. Their role in promoting Reno and its industries helped create a favorable climate for ventures like mine.
